coredata.dc:title: The use of chitosan as a bioactive polysaccharide in non-invasive detection of malondialdehyde biomarker in human exhaled breath condensate: A new platform towards diagnosis of some lung disease
coredata.dc:creator: Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Babaie, Parinaz
Jouyban-Gharamaleki, Vahid
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a reusable and time-saving strategy for the electrochemical polymerization of dopamine (EPD) is reported. In this work, biocompatible polymer chitosan (CS) was electrodeposited on the surface of PDA modified glassy carbon electrode. Owing to the abundant catechol and amine groups in the PDA layer, uniform silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) are deposited onto the POLY(DA-CS) and can effectively prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs generated from photo-electrocatalysis and transfer the captured electrons to participate in the photo-electrocatalytic reaction process. Compared with pristine POLY(DA-CS), the as-prepared POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs organic-inorganic hybrid exhibit electrochemical sensitivity for detecting MDA and display excellent durability. Furthermore, the mussel-inspired electropolymerization strategy and the fast EPD-Ag nanoparticle decorating process presented herein can be readily extended to various biomedical analysis and lung cancer detection. It is the adaptation of the established POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs organic-inorganic hybrid for a selective, robust, and generalizable sensing system that is the emphasis of this work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioactive polysaccharide
Malondialdehyde
Oxidative stress
Lipid peroxidation
Biomarker analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of exhaled breath condensate (EBC) samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Institutes for Medical Research Development
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Institute for Medical Research Development

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Research reported in this publication was supported by Elite Researcher Grant Committee under award number [962147] from the National Institutes for Medical Research Development (NIMAD), Tehran, Iran. Also, we thanks Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research.

Research reported in this publication was supported by Elite Researcher Grant Committee under award number [ 962147 ] from the National Institutes for Medical Research Development (NIMAD), Tehran, Iran. Also, we thanks Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) CVs for 0.005 g dopamine containing 5 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) using GCE (no cycle = 15, sweep rate is 100 mV/s, potential range is; −1 to +1 V vs. AgAgCl). B) CVs for 02 g/L CS in 0.1 M HCl (no cycle = 30, sweep rate is 100 mV/s, potential range is; −1 to +1 V vs. AgAgCl) using POLY(DA)-GCE. C) CVs of POLY(DA-CS)-GCE in 1.5 mM aqueous solution of AgNO3 in HNO3 (the range of potential applied was −1 to 0.5 V vs. Ag/AgCl at room temperature, sweep rate was 100 mV/s).
FE-SEM images of Ag NPs electrodeposited on the surface of Poly(DA)-GCE (A), Poly(DA-CS)-GCE (B), Poly(DA-CS)-Ag NPs (C) in different magnification.
Typical CVs of GCE (A), POLY(DA)-GCE (B), POLY(DA-CS)-GCE (C), and POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE (D), in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 0.1 M MDA. Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4).

A) CVs of POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE in the presence of 0.1 M MDA in different scan rates (10, 100, 200, 300,400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, and 1000 mV/s). Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M PBS. B) Dependency of peak current to the square root of sweep rates.

A) SWVs of POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE in different concentrations of MDA in the standard with different concentrations (1.45, 1.49, 1.53, 1.58, 1.68, 1.73, 1.79, 1.85, 1.68, 1.73, 1.79, 1.85, 1.92, 1.99, 2.07, 2.15, 2.24, 2.34, 2.45, 2.57, 2.70, 2.84, 3.00, 3.18, 3.38, 3.61, 3.88, 4.18, 4.54, 4.96, 4.47, 6.09, 7.48, and 7.9 μM). Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M PBS (pH = 10.0). Inset is calibration curves. B) DPVs of POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE in different concentrations of MDA in the EBC samples with different concentrations (0.817, 0.837, 0.857, 0.87, 0.90, 0.92, 0.95, 0.97, 1.00, 1.03, 1.06, 1.09, 1.13, 1.17, 1.21, 1.25, 1.30, 1.35, 1.40, 1.52, 1.59, 1.67, 1.75, 1.85, 1.95, 2.06, 2.19, 2.34, 2.51, 2.70, 2.93, 3.19, and 3.51 μM). Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M PBS (pH = 10.0). Inset is calibration curves.
Possible mechanism for electropolymerization of POLY(DA).
Possible mechanism for electropolymerization of POLY(DA-CS) and electrodeposition of Ag NPs.
Possible oxidation mechanism of MDA using poly(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE.
Analytical parameters determined for calibration curves of MDA in the standard samples using POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE.
Analytical parameters determined for calibration curves of MDA in the human EBC samples using POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE.
Analytical parameters for detection of MDA at different methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: HPLC; high performance liquid chromatography, LC-MS/MS; high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy/mass spectroscopy, SPR; surface plasmon spectroscopy, CE; capillary electrophoresis, GC; Gas Chromatography, EC; Electrochem. EBC.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Inter-day precision, accuracy and recoveries of the method for assay of MDA.
Intra-day precision, accuracy and recoveries for determination of MDA.
Details of the stability of the proposed method in EBC samples.

head.ce:title: The use of chitosan as a bioactive polysaccharide in non-invasive detection of malondialdehyde biomarker in human exhaled breath condensate: A new platform towards diagnosis of some lung disease

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechatronic Engineering, International Campus, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a reusable and time-saving strategy for the electrochemical polymerization of dopamine (EPD) is reported. In this work, biocompatible polymer chitosan (CS) was electrodeposited on the surface of PDA modified glassy carbon electrode. Owing to the abundant catechol and amine groups in the PDA layer, uniform silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) are deposited onto the POLY(DA-CS) and can effectively prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs generated from photo-electrocatalysis and transfer the captured electrons to participate in the photo-electrocatalytic reaction process. Compared with pristine POLY(DA-CS), the as-prepared POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs organic-inorganic hybrid exhibit electrochemical sensitivity for detecting MDA and display excellent durability. Furthermore, the mussel-inspired electropolymerization strategy and the fast EPD-Ag nanoparticle decorating process presented herein can be readily extended to various biomedical analysis and lung cancer detection. It is the adaptation of the established POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs organic-inorganic hybrid for a selective, robust, and generalizable sensing system that is the emphasis of this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxidative stress is caused by an imbalance between oxidants and antioxidants, and can include cellular or individual levels. The results may include oxidative damage and oxidative changes in macromolecules at the cellular level, inducing cell death (apoptosis) or necrosis and tissue damage. This process has been widely studied. Radical hydroxyl (OH⁎) is one of the most important radicals species in oxidative stress. Due to the high reactivity of radical hydroxyl, there is no antioxidant to detoxify it, and all biological molecules can be attacked by it. One of these reactions is lipid peroxidation. In this process unsaturated fatty acids are involved which is associated with degradation of the cell membrane and if not repaired, it leads to necrosis. Cell membrane is a rich source of unsaturated fatty acids [1–4].

Lipid peroxidation has many harmful effects on biological systems, such as aging, cancer, cardiovascular and liver disease, rheumatoid arthritis, inflammation, diabetes mellitus, age related macular degeneration, acute Pulmonary disorders, Parkinson's, Alzheimer's and Schizophrenia [5–9]. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) is an oxidative stress biomarker but due to its high reactivity and very short half-life, it is impossible to direct measurement in tissue and body fluids. Therefore, the measurement of the final products of peroxidation of lipids and proteins has attracted much attention to study ROS effect as oxidative stress biomarkers [2,10]. Beside lipid peroxides cytotoxicity, it also increase the signaling associated with inflammatory responses [11]. Malondialdehyde (MDA) is the main reactive aldehyde that is produce due to cell membrane peroxidation, which is used as a biomarker for tissue damage manifestation [12,13]. MDA has been widely studied as a peroxidation product [9,11,14,15]. MDA ability to interact with functional groups of various cellular compositions, including amine groups, SH groups in sulfhydryl compounds and nucleic acids bases can be effective in the progression of tumors [6]. In addition to oxidation of unsaturated fatty acids, which are the main source of MDA production in in vivo environments. Other minor sources, such as byproduct of prostaglandins biosynthesis, also contributes to its production [1].

MDA is a potential indicator of some health-related acute problems, such as cancer of lung, breast, cervical, stomach and skin. It has also been proven that MDA levels in living organisms are affected by pathological conditions [16]. High levels of MDA have been observed in several biological fluids samples taken from patients with respiratory system diseases such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and bronchiectasis [11,17,18]. It is also known as biomarker of cancer, pulmonary, cardiovascular, liver and psychiatric diseases [14,19,20].

COPD refers to the constraint of continuous airflow. The disease is preventable and curable. The limitation of the flow of air is progressive and is accompanied by increasing the chronic inflammatory response of the lung to harmful particles and gases. The methods used to identify the markers of this pulmonary disease are often invasive and include cases such as blood sampling, induced sputum and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid. These methods may induce inflammatory responses, hence a non-invasive approach has been attended such as exhaled breath condensate (EBC) [10,18].

EBC is obtained via the exhaust air cooling and has recently been proven to be a reliable biologic tool for monitoring oxidative stress and low respiratory tract disturbances. During the studies on EBC, several biological markers such as aldehydes, H2O2, adenosine, leukotriene and cytokines have been measured in this biological sample and further studies have reported on the use of EBC in relation to several diseases [11,12]. Various techniques have been used to detect MDA in various biological samples. Methods include ultraviolet-visible, fluorescence, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, high performance liquid chromatography, and Thiobarbituric acid (TBA) assay with diode array detection, gas chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, electron capture detection and electrochemical biosensors [9,16,19,21,22]. One of important methods are electrochemical sensing based on advanced nanomaterial.
The literature survey reveals that there is one report on the electroanalytical method for the determination of MDA [23].

The performance of the electrochemical sensors is based on the reaction with the analyte to produce the appropriate electrical signal related to concentration of analyte. Electrochemical biosensors benefit from the advantages of easy to use, inexpensive and the ability to rapidly measure and analyze the concentration of analyte in complex samples, which have attracted much attention and application in the medical diagnosis [24]. Furthermore, the use of a wide range of nanomaterials, especially nanoparticles in analytical methods has many advantages and applications [25].

In the last two decades, the preparation of modified electrodes has attracted a lot of attention to the use of electro-analysis by the immobilization of electron mediators on the surface of electrodes. Recent studies have demonstrated that electropolymerization has received great attention because of its simplicity, selectivity and sensitivity towards analytes, strong adherence of polymer film to electrode surface and ability to provide larger surface area by forming homogeneous membrane [26]. Various interface based on polymers have been described in the literatures for the development of some electrochemical sensors [27–33]. Moreover, the combined of these polymers with nanoparticles make them attractive for the electrochemical analysis.
One of important polymers is poly dopamine. Most recently, we developed a facile and effective one step electropolymerization method for the preparation of PDA-β-CD on the surface of GCE [34].

After some of experimental study by our research group in this area [34], we found the intensity of peak current obtained by PDA and PDA-β-CD in electrochemical sensor is low. Also, stability of CDs on the surface of electrode is very small. Therefore, we need to use some of metals such as gold, iron, silver, and palladium to enhance the peak currents which is necessary for determination trace amount of analytes in real samples. So, in the present study, electrochemically synthesized silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are used for enhancing the signal for electrochemical application. On the other hand we need to use a biocompatible and stable compound for the increasing of electrode durability. Therefore, chitosan was selected as the best candidate for this purpose. Biocompatible polymer chitosan (CS) produced by treating shrimp and other crustacean shells with the alkali sodium hydroxide has been extensively used in sensor technology. Due to its attractive properties, that includes excellent film-forming ability, high permeability, good adhesion, nontoxicity, cheapness and a susceptibility to chemical modification. It also facilitates the electron transfer after its swelling in the reaction mixture due to its hydrophilic nature [35–37]. Besides, chitosan can be used as a dispersant to form stable composite which can form a stable film on the electrode surface [38]. CS as a polysaccharide biopolymer, which displays excellent film-forming ability, high water permeability, good adhesion, and susceptibility to chemical modifications due to the presence of reactive amino and hydroxyl functional groups.

Nanoparticles provide suitable substrates for bonding various biological molecules. Nanomaterials have been in the center of consideration due to electrical conductivity and high surface area, wide electrochemical substrate, excellent adhesion of molecules, chemical stability and electrochemical and physical properties. The polymer films used to modify electrodes significantly increase the electro-analytical capabilities, reaction rate and improve the stability of the electrode's reaction [39,40].

While the electrochemical synthesis of AgNPs is well established [41,42], the scheme presented herein enables particularly short deposition times, minimal solution preparation, and no requirement of an external linker or functionalizing ligand. Moreover, in-situ tailoring of the nanoparticle surface chemistry resulted in an improved affinity and selectivity. Therefore, Poly(dopamine)-Chitosan (POLY(DA)-CS) was used as an organic matric for stabilization of AgNPs on the surface of GCE. So, the final nanocomposite was POLY(DA)-CS-AgNPs. This nanocomposite (POLY(DA)-CS-AgNPs) were used for electrochemical sensing of MDA using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV).

In summary, these improved performances encouraged us to explore the possible leading role played by the presence of AgNPs on the structure of POLY(DA)-CS. The proposed nanocomposite (POLY(DA)-CS-AgNPs) increases the contact area with the analyte (MDA biomarker), so they could increase the electrochemical active surface to interact with MDA. Since the increase in geometric surface area is very important parameter in electrochemistry, therefore modification of POLY(DA)-CS with AgNPs can increase the rate of electrochemical reaction. Therefore, integration of AgNPs into POLY(DA)-CS can be providing an electrically conductive film towards increases Faradic currents. Excellent properties of POLY(DA)-CS and the advantages of AgNPs have received more attention for preparation of high performance electrode material. It was for these reasons that we decided to explore the possibility of using the POLY(DA)-CS-AgNPs as a platform for the development of electrochemical sensor for detection of MDA. Finally, we investigated the fully electrochemical preparation of POLY(DA)-CS-AgNPs on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as a novel strategy for interface science. The formation of POLY(DA)-CS-AgNPs was firstly monitored. The latent activity of P-DA-CS-AgNPs coating towards detection and determination of MDA was then investigated. The redox behavior of prepared nano-sensor was then characterized by CV and DPV. Then, we used POLY(DA)-CS-AgNPs as a new interface for electro-oxidation and determination of MDA in human exhaled breath condensate. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the determination of MDA based on their direct electrochemical oxidation on POLY(DA)-CS-AgNPs films.

All chemicals used in this work were of analytical reagent grade. Dopamine, chitosan, Silver Nitrate (AgNO3), citric acid, HNO3, NaOH, and MDA were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Additional dilute solutions were prepared daily by accurate dilution just before use. MDA (produced from in situ hydrolysis of H2SO4) solutions were stable, and their concentrations did not change with time. Di-potassium phosphate and mono-potassium phosphate were obtained from Scharlau. Deionized water was purchased from Ghazi Pharmaceutical Company (Tabriz, Iran). Stock standard solution of MDA (0.1 mM) was prepared by dissolving an appropriate mass of sulfuric acid in PBS buffer (pH 7.4), and the daily standard working solutions of different concentrations were prepared by diluting the stock solution with buffer and were freshly used.

Electrochemical experiments were performed with a computer-controlled AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands), driven with NOVA 1.7 software. A conventional three-electrode cell was used with an Ag/AgCl (Metrohm, The Netherlands) as a reference electrode, and a Pt wire was used as a counter-electrode. The working electrode was GCE (d = 2 mm) (from Azar electrode Co., Iran). For DPV measurements, a pulse width of 25 mV, a pulse time of 50 ms, and a scan rate of 50 mV/s were employed. The surface morphology of the modified electrodes was evaluated with a MIRA3 FEG-SEM (SEM, Hitachi Ltd., Czech).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of exhaled breath condensate (EBC) samples
ce:section.ce:para: EBC samples were obtained from healthy volunteers using a lab-made setup based on a cooling trap system patented in the national patent office. The setup cools down the exhaled breath down to −25 °C and condenses the EBC with acceptable efficiency. 1.0 mL EBC sample collected from a healthy volunteer was transferred to a 10.0 mL volumetric flask, a standard solution of desired concentration was added up to the mark line, and electrochemical analysis was performed.

GCE (2 mm in diameter) was polished to a mirror-like finish with 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina slurry (Buehler, USA) followed by rinsing thoroughly with double distilled water. Then it was successively sonicated in acetone and double distilled water, and allowed to dry at room temperature.

CV was used to form the preparation thin film of Poly(DA) on the surface of GCE. Fig. 1A presents 15 consecutively CV of GCE in the presence of 0.005 g dopamine containing 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) in the range of −1.0 to 1.0 V with scan rate of 100 mV/s. Also, for the preparation thin film of Poly(DA) on the surface of GCE, homogenous dopamine (0.005 g) was added to 5 cm3 PBS (pH = 7.4) and transferred to electrochemical cell. Then Poly(DA) films were electrodeposited onto GCE by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the potential range from −1.0 to 1.0 V at a scan rate of 100 mV/s for 15 cycles (Fig. 1A). The first positive scan, an oxidation peak at +0.28 V was observed, which is attributed to the oxidation of the DA. In the subsequent reversal scan, two reduction peaks were observed at +0.23 V and −0.16 V, which may be attributed to the reduction of dopaminequinone (DAQ) and dopaminechrome (DAC) as well as the intramolecular cyclization reaction product of dopaminequinone (DAQ). In the second positive scan, a new oxidation peak (a2) appeared at ca. −0.12 V, which may be attributed to the oxidation of leucodopaminechrome (LDAC). Simultaneously, the peak current of peak (a1) and peak decreased cycle by cycle, and other peak current continuously increased with continuous scanning, reflecting the continuous growth of the film. When the cyclic potential scan reached 15 cycles, the peak currents hardly changed. These facts indicate that DA was successfully polymerized on the surface of GCE by the electropolymerization method. The polymerization mechanism of DA is described as follows: dopaminechrome (DAC) molecules partly may evolve to the more stable 5,6‑dihydroindole molecules of two aromatic rings via isomerization, and 5,6‑indolequinone is then electropolymerized to form polymers [43]. Scheme 1 shows electropolymerization of POLY(DA) at the surface of GCE.

It has been confirmed that monomer containing NH2 can be electropolymerized at the surface of electrode to form a conducting film via covalent bond (CN) between electrode and NH2. DA has two NH2 and two NH groups and that can easily be oxidized to produce NH2. Covalent bond (CN) is then formed by the free radical reaction of NH2 with GCE surface with excellent stability. Therefore, the covalent bond (CN) might be formed between carbon electrode and the α-amino group of dopamine. It is important to point out that, during the high potential scan rate, the α-amino free radical will easily form and the polymerization of the poly amino acid film could be formed on the surface of GCE.

At the second step for preparation of modified electrode, the three-electrode system was transferred into 0.1 M HCl containing 2 g/L CS, and 20 repetitive cycle in the potential range from −0.1 to 0.15 V vs. Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 100 mV/s. Therefore, POLY(DA-CS)-GCE was prepared in this step. As we know, the carbocyclic functional group on POLY(DA) are beneficial to the combination with CS from the electrostatic point of view, since CS dissolved in acid (HCl in the present work) is positively charged. Therefore, at the final modification step for organic interface, CS was deposited on the surface of POLY(DA) from aqueous solution in the potential range from −1 to 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 100 mV/s Using cyclic voltammetry techniques (Fig. 1B). So, the results show that the hydrophilic CS can interact with POLY(DA) via CO2 − and NH3 + side groups (amid), which enable forming distinctive assembly network between the CS chains. Finally, thin film of POLY(DA-CS) was obtained at the surface of GCE (see Scheme 2 ).

Finally, The AgNPs were electrochemically synthesized at the surface POLY(DA-CS) modified GCE using CV technique. The electrochemical solution consisted of 1.5 mM aqueous solution of AgNO3 in HNO3, while range of potential applied was −1 to 0.5 V vs. Ag/AgCl at room temperature (Fig. 1B). Thus synthesized nanocomposite are referred as POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE throughout the text. After deposition, the electrodes were washed with distilled water and dried in air. The modified electrode was denoted as POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE.

FEG-SEM images of GCE, POLY(DA)-GCE, POLY(DA-CS)-GCE, and POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE are shown in Figs. 2–4
. POLY(DA) electropolymerized directly on GCE in the absence of CS as matrix displays a regular dispersion (Fig. 2A). If the POLY(DA)-GCE was coated with CS beforehand, an irregular and spongy film can be observed (Fig. 2B) which the following deposition of Ag NPs on the POLY(DA-CS)-GCE results in the formation of an interwoven chain-like structure of POLY(DA-CS)-GCE with regularly morphology (Fig. 2B), providing an efficient interface for the following dispersion of Ag NPs. Finally, these results confirmed that the GCE was coated by POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE film, leading to the change in the surface morphology (highly porous honeycomb-like structure) and activity of the electrode. As mentioned above, the hydroxyl functional groups of POLY(DA) are beneficial to the combination with CS from the electrostatic point of view, since CS dissolved in acid (HCl in the present work) is positively charged. Fig. 2C indicated that, the electrodeposition of Ag NPs on the surface of POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs lead to spongy structures. Also, these results confirm that dispersion of Ag NPs on the surface of POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs was performed successfully. As can be seen, the average particle size of Ag NPs are about 35 nm. All of these results were confirmed by EDS analysis of prepared interface (Fig. 3). The POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs nanocomposites exhibit an interconnected and highly porous honeycomb-like structure (Fig. 2C), suggesting that the nanocomposites can be promising materials for entrapment of MDA. More importantly, excellent dispersion of the Ag NPs is clearly observed within the POLY(DA-CS) matrix (Fig. 2B), indicating that the POLY(DA-CS) nanocomposites prepared via simple and convenient assembly exhibit a well-dispersed status due to the strong interactions, electrostatic attraction and H bonding, between P(DA) and CS. It is important to point out that, pure CS would be dissolved in aqueous solution at pH below 6.3 due to the protonation of its NH2 groups. So in this work pure CS cannot be used to modify GCE individually for the determination of MDA in 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.4. However, the strong electrostatic interactions between positively charged CS and negatively charged P(DA) result in the aggregation and subsequent preparation of the P(DA-CS), leading to stable existence of the nanocomposites for dispersion of Ag NPs over a wide pH range (4.5–11.0). It should be pointed out extreme acidity (pH < 4.5) or alkalinity (pH > 11.0) can cause the dissociation of the P(DA-CS) owing to the blocked coulomb forces between the two components and the destroyed condensed state of the complex by the increased local charge density. Anyway, the enhanced stability of the P(DA-CS) over the pH range from 4.5 to 11.0 compared with pure CS and p(DA) makes the nanocomposites an ideal substrate for immobilization of noble metal nanoparticle (Ag NPs in this case) to be used in the PBS buffer of pH 7.4.

The voltammograms of bare GCE, POLY(DA-CS)-GCE, POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE in the absence and presence of MDA were shown in Fig. 4 which the supporting electrolyte was 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4). As seen in Fig. 4A any oxidation signal was not exhibited on the GCE. This indicated the electro-inactivity of MDA on the GC surface. Typical CVs of POLY(DA-CS)-GCE in 0.1 M PBS is shown as Fig. 6B where potential sweep rate of 100 mV/s has been employed. In the absence of MDA, no peak appears at the POLY(DA-CS)-GCE. While MDA exhibits one redox peak which, the anodic peak potential and peak current for the oxidation of MDA at POLY(DA-CS)-GCE is about 0.36713 V and 13.2 μA, respectively. Interestingly, the data obtained clearly show that the combination of POLY(DA) and CS definitely improve the characteristics of MDA oxidation (low oxidation potential and high peak current). That might be related to the excellent property of POLY(DA-CS), such as high specific surface area and electrical conductivity. The role of POLY(DA-CS) was also important for promoting the electrochemical oxidation of MDA. So POLY(DA-CS)-GCE could be substituted for the oxidation of MDA. Fig. 4C shows the CVs of POLY(DA-CS)-GCE in the absence and presence of 0.1 mM MDA. The current response of MDA at the POLY(DA-CS)-GCE indicating that this organic hybrid possesses remarkable enhancement effects on the oxidation of MDA. It is noteworthy that the present response of MDA was dramatically improved when CS was introduced to the surface of POLY(DA). This can attribute to hydrogen bonding between the POLY(DA-CS) and MDA. This leads to a greater amount of MDA accumulation on the surface of POLY(DA-CS)-GCE. Therefore MDA could be more accumulated on the electrode surface due to their interaction with POLY(DA-CS).

According to the obtained results, the intensity of peak currents obtained by POLY(DA-CS)-GCE is low (13.2 μA). Therefore, we need to use metals such as silver which no requirement of an external linker or functionalization. Fig. 4D compares the CV, and SWV responses of a POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE in the absence and presence of MDA. It is observed that a pair of redox peaks of the POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE was appeared, which confirmed electrodeposition of AgNPs on the surface of the POLY(DA-CS). While MDA exhibits one redox peak which, the anodic peak potential and peak current for the oxidation of MDA at POLY(DA-CS)-GCE is about 0.63812 V and 97.4 μA, respectively. This is related to synergetic effect of P(DA-CS) and Ag NPs on the signal amplification. We suggest that the redox peaks correspond to the electrocatalytical nature of AgNPs on the surface of the pre-modified electrode. The reason of this approach is low peak current in the absence of MDA by POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE (Ipa = 13.2 μA).

In summary, the stepwise assembly of two materials on the GCE is accompanied by a change in the voltammetric response and a variation of the peak to peak separation between the cathodic and anodic waves (ΔE
P ) of the redox probe. An irreversible redox behavior of MDA was observed on the bare GCE with a peak-to-peak separation of 0.62 V. Importantly, the response at the POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE was similar to the shape obtained on other modified electrode, and in addition, the redox peak current increased and the ΔE
P decreased with Ag NPs assembly. We concluded that this is caused by the Ag NPs. First, NPs possess a concentration effect, a directional effect and a quantum size effect. They decrease the resistance of electron transfer and increase the electron transfer rate. Second, Ag NPs exhibit relatively good electron conductivity. Third, the high surface-to-volume ratio of the assembled NPs materials may greatly enhance the density of the sensor. These voltammograms showed that the Ag NPs were successfully assembled on the POLY(DA-CS) and, as for the electron transfer, it was better than the POLY(DA-CS)-GCE. This may be ascribed to the electrostatic effect of MDA and Ag NPs. Based on above discussed electrodes, POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE has greater interaction with MDA. Hence, higher peak current of MDA is expected because of existence of Ag NPs on the structure of POLY(DA-CS). Therefore, the reason of this phenomena is synergetic effect of POLY(DA), CS, and catalytically role of AgNPs.

Scan rate is one of important items affecting electroactivity of various organic molecules. The electrooxidation of MDA at various scan rates was investigated on the surface of POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE by CV technique. The effect of the scan rate on electrooxidation of MDA was studied in the range from 10 to 1000 mV/s using CV technique (Fig. 5A). The effect of variation of scan rate was studied using POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE in 0.1 M MDA while supporting electrolyte was 0.1 M NaOH of pH 12. Based on Fig. 5B, oxidation peak currents (Ipa) of MDA were found to be proportional to the square root of scan rates with the linear regression equation. This shows oxidation peak currents (Ipa) of MDA is proportional to square root of scan rates of scan rate, indicating that the electrode reaction of MDA at POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE is controlled by the diffusion process [44]. Also, the plot of oxidation peak potentials versus Napierian logarithm of scan rates (lnυ) exhibited a linear relationship. For an irreversible process, Epa is defined by Laviron's equation [45]:
(1)
E pa
=
RT / 2
1 − α
nF
lnυ + K
where α is the electron transfer coefficient and n is the number of electrons involved in the rate-determining step. Other symbols have their usual meanings. The term RT/2(1 − α)nF in Eq. (1) represents the slope of plot, which equals to 0.021 for MDA biomarker. Assuming the electron transfer coefficient α to be 0.5 for an irreversible electrode process, the number of electrons transferred was found to be 1.11 for MDA electrooxidation. According to these results, the possible mechanism for electrooxidation of MDA was shown in Scheme 3 .

The SWV is an effective and a rapid electroanalytical technique with lower concentration detection limits. The SWV was employed for the determination of MDA in the standard and human plasma samples. Fig. 6A displays the SWV response of the POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE for increasing MDA concentrations under the optimum experimental conditions. One well-defined oxidation peaks were observed. Meanwhile, a good linear relationship between the peak currents and the MDA concentration was obtained. The peak currents were proportional to MDA concentration in the range of 1.45–7.9 μM. The low limit of quantification (LLOQ) was estimated to be 1.45 μM for MDA. The low detection limit of the POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE could be ascribed to the high loading of AgNPs and POLY(DA-CS) by the present method and the rapid electron transfer between MDA and the POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE. The comparison of POLY(DA-CS)-AgNPs-GCE with other modified electrodes for MDA detection is shown in Table 1 . From the Table 1 it can be seen that this method had the comparable sensitivity, detection limit and wide linear range for the detection with the simple electrode preparation procedure. Thus, this method allowed to sensitive determination of MDA.

For detection of MDA in EBC of healthy EBC sample, four concentrations of MDA were applied by the standard addition method and obtained results are given in Table 2 and Fig. 6B. To test the accuracy of this electrochemical method, the results were compared with the standard method and summarized in Table 2. It was obvious that the MDA content detected by proposed method was lower than that detected by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry. It may be due to rather poor sensitivity and selectivity of ultraviolet-visible detection for MDA. Therefore, this method could be further developed for the clinical assay of intracorporeal MDA content.

Electrode stability related to the number of cycles was also checked. As obtained, with increase of the number of cycles to 100, the peak currents decrease very smoothly with error of 2.1% where in higher cycles is decreased remarkably, for example, in 100 cycles error percent is 10%. Results approve that the electrode is stable at least 100 replicate analysis.
Finally, Table 3 compares the results of this work with previously reported methods.

FDA validation guidelines introduces the inter-day and intra-day evaluation methods to show the precision of the reported method where three concentrations (low, mid and high) of MDA were tested. For intra-day and inter-day precision, 4 replicated analyses of EBC of MDA concentrations of 0.1, 0.3 and 0.8 μM were performed.

Tables 4 and 5
had shown the inter-day and intra-day precision, accuracy and recovery of the method in of plasma and whole blood samples. Concerning the maximum FDA permitted coefficient of variation value of 15%, the method has acceptable accuracy and precision.

These data revealed that the introduced method had an accepted level of precision. Recoveries varied from 89.2 to 103.7% by equating the calculated content with the nominal concentration of MDA. The experimental outcomes disclosed that the developed method was practical for the analysis of MDA in human whole blood and plasma samples.

The aim of stability testing procedures is to clear the ways that whether the analyte is stable to environmental change including the matrix and storage conditions or not. Herein, we tested the short time temperature and freeze-thaw stability of the method to analysis of MDA in whole blood and plasma samples. As FDA recommended experimental procedure of short time temperature, we selected two different concentrations of MDA across the dynamic range and left the samples to stand at room temperature for at least 24 h before analysis. Results showed that the differences between the values measured in fresh samples and the prepared short time temperature samples were in the FDA allowed range. These results reflect that the MDA aliquots were stable at room temperature for at least 24 h. Also, freeze-thaw stability of the method were tested using whole blood and plasma aliquots containing MDA concentrations of 0.1 and 0.8 μM where the three FDA recommended freeze-thaw cycles were tested. As clear in Table 6 , the proposed analytical method possesses the allowed variations of lower 15% for whole plasma and whole blood samples.

We have successfully designed a new platform based on Au NPs and POLY(DA-CS) for sensitive voltammetric detection of MDA in EBC samples. POLY(DA-CS)-Ag NPs film were prepared by the full electrochemically method onto the surface of glassy carbon electrodes. Ag NPs were covalently bonded to POLY(DA-CS) films and used to bind of MDA biomarker. The novel detection scheme is a suitable electrochemical assay, which makes the MDA detection simple and rapid. The engineered electrochemical sensor showed a linear dynamic range and low limit of quantification limit of 1.45–7.9 μM and 1.45 μM, respectively. The proposed sensor showed excellent analytical performance for the detection of MDA with high sensitivity and reproducibility. The prepared sensor detected MDA from EBC samples with high recovery values. We conclude that this platform has the potential to be used in clinical applications and point-of-care diagnosis of lung disease and specially cancer a simple and rapid assay.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Research reported in this publication was supported by Elite Researcher Grant Committee under award number [962147] from the National Institutes for Medical Research Development (NIMAD), Tehran, Iran. Also, we thanks Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research.
